slides.title: Python Has a Style Rulebook
slides.body: **PEP 8** is Python's official style guide — a shared set of rules so every Python developer's code looks familiar. Think of it as the grammar handbook for the Python language.
slides.tip: PEP stands for Python Enhancement Proposal. PEP 8 (from 2001) is the one that defines style.

slides.title: Indent with 4 Spaces, Always
slides.body: Python uses indentation to define code blocks. PEP 8 says: **4 spaces per level**, every time. Never tabs, never 2 spaces.
slides.tip: Set your editor to insert 4 spaces when you press Tab — then you never have to think about it.

slides.title: Names Signal What Things Are
slides.body: Use `snake_case` for variables and functions, `PascalCase` for classes, and `UPPER_CASE` for constants. The style tells you what you're looking at before you read the name.

slides.title: Give Operators Room to Breathe
slides.body: Put **one space on each side** of operators. Cramped code is hard to scan — spaced code reads like math on a whiteboard.
slides.code:
# Good
x = 5 + 3
result = x * 2 - 1

# Bad
x=5+3
result=x*2-1
slides.tip: Exception: no spaces around `=` in keyword arguments — write `print(end="")` not `print(end = "")`.

slides.title: Let Tools Enforce Style for You
slides.body: You don't need to memorize every rule. Run **`black`** to auto-format your file instantly, or **`ruff`** to highlight style problems. Most teams run these automatically.
slides.code:
# In your terminal (not Python):
# pip install black
# black my_script.py
slides.tip: VS Code and PyCharm can run black automatically every time you save — search for "format on save".

slides.title: Style is a Team Superpower
slides.body: Consistent code means new teammates read it without a guide, code reviews focus on **logic not formatting**, and bugs have fewer places to hide. *Readability counts* — that's the Python Zen.
slides.tip: Type `import this` in Python to read the Zen of Python — the philosophy behind PEP 8.
